explicit_text: I was born in Grass Valley, California on April 7, 1934. It’s where my story begins before I moved on to other places for work and union involvement. My early roots in Grass Valley shaped my upbringing before I eventually joined the workforce and unions elsewhere.
implicit_text: I was born in a small city in northern California on April 7, 1934. It’s where my story begins before I moved on to other places for work and union involvement. My early roots in that town shaped my upbringing before I eventually joined the workforce and unions elsewhere.

explicit_text: I was a member of the International Longshore and Warehouse Union, Local 63, for 35 years and worked on the waterfront for a total of about 44 years. I’m currently involved with the pensioners group in southern California and serve on its executive board. The ILWU became a big part of my life after I transitioned from working at Shell Chemical and logging in Oregon.
implicit_text: I was a member of a prominent West Coast waterfront union, Local 63, for 35 years and worked on the docks for a total of about 44 years. I’m currently involved with the pensioners group in southern California and serve on its executive board. This waterfront union became a big part of my life after I transitioned from working at a chemical company and logging in Oregon.

explicit_text: Before joining the ILWU, I worked for Shell Chemical for ten years. I was very active in the Oil, Chemical and Atomic Workers International Union during that time, even serving as secretary of our local group. My time at Shell was marked by a lot of union activity and clashes with the company, which eventually led me to leave and work on the waterfront.
implicit_text: Before joining the waterfront union, I worked for a major international oil and chemical company for ten years. I was very active in the union representing oil, chemical, and atomic workers during that time, even serving as secretary of our local group. My time at that company was marked by a lot of union activity and clashes with management, which eventually led me to leave and work on the waterfront.

entity: Oil, Chemical and Atomic Workers International Union
explicit_text: While working at Shell Chemical, I was very active in the Oil, Chemical and Atomic Workers International Union. Our plant was 100 percent unionized, and I served as secretary of the union group for several years. This union experience was important to me before I moved to the waterfront and joined the ILWU.
implicit_text: While working at the chemical company, I was very active in the union representing oil, chemical, and atomic workers. Our plant was 100 percent unionized, and I served as secretary of the union group for several years. This union experience was important to me before I moved to the waterfront and joined the longshore union.

explicit_text: I served in the United States Army from 1954 to 1955. After my discharge, I went to Oregon to work with my brother in logging. My time in the Army was a brief but important chapter before I started my civilian working life in Oregon and later in California.
implicit_text: I served in the U.S. military from 1954 to 1955. After my discharge, I went to Oregon to work with my brother in logging. My time in the service was a brief but important chapter before I started my civilian working life in Oregon and later in California.

explicit_text: After leaving the Army in 1955, I went to Oregon to work in logging with my brother Ken near Glendale. I worked as a choker setter behind his Caterpillar machinery for about a year until the woods closed down. Oregon was the place where I first worked after military service before moving to southern California.
implicit_text: After leaving the military in 1955, I went to the Pacific Northwest to work in logging with my brother Ken near Glendale. I worked as a choker setter behind his Caterpillar machinery for about a year until the woods closed down. That state was the place where I first worked after military service before moving to southern California.

explicit_text: My brother Chuck worked in Stockton, California for three years after high school starting in 1949. He talked a lot about working on ships and about Harry Bridges, which gave me some early knowledge about the ILWU. Stockton was part of my family’s connection to the waterfront and union work before I joined the ILWU myself.
implicit_text: My brother Chuck worked in a Central Valley city in California for three years after high school starting in 1949. He talked a lot about working on ships and about a famous labor leader, which gave me some early knowledge about the waterfront union. That city was part of my family’s connection to the docks and union work before I joined the longshore union myself.

explicit_text: I first heard about Harry Bridges from my brother Chuck, who worked on ships and spoke about him often. Bridges was a significant figure in the ILWU, and knowing about him made me think the union would be a good place to work. Although I didn’t know much about the ILWU before moving to southern California, Harry Bridges’ reputation was part of what drew me in.
